radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT LOW DOSE LUNG SCREENING

INDICATION:  ___ yr old, former smoker (1), 40 pk yrs, asymptomatic// Ct Lung
Cancer Screening  Baseline      Ct Lung Cancer Screening  Baseline

TECHNIQUE:  Volumetric CT acquisitions over the entire thorax in inspiration,
no administration of intravenous contrast material, multiplanar
reconstructions. The technical details of the protocol are consistent with the
___ College of Radiology (___) requirements for low-dose CT lung cancer
screening*

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 6.0 s, 38.9 cm; CTDIvol = 5.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 201.3
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 201 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:
Baseline screening CT.

Comparison to prior CT chest done on ___

FINDINGS:


Neck, thoracic inlet, axillae:
No abnormality.

Breast, chest wall and bones:
No abnormality

Mediastinum:
Small mediastinal lymph nodes not enlarged by size criteria

Hila:
No abnormalities

Heart:
No abnormalities

Upper Abdomen:
Status post cholecystectomy.  Trace pneumobilia

Lung:

Nodules:

Dominant nodule:
None

Other nodules:
Stable 2 mm calcified nodule in the right upper lobe (5, 103).
Stable 1 mm solid right middle lobe pulmonary nodule (5, 145.

Stable 1 mm right middle lobe pulmonary nodule (5, 147).
Stable 1 mm solid left lower lobe pulmonary nodule (5, 175).

Parenchyma:
Mild upper lobe predominant emphysema.

Pleura and airways:
No abnormalities


IMPRESSION: 

Stable tiny pulmonary nodules ranging in size from 1-2 mm.

Mild upper lobe predominant emphysema.

Lung-RADS category: 2

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Continue low-dose lung cancer screening CT in 12 months.

Incidental findings**:
None



___ Radiology is an ACR accredited CT lung cancer screening site.
**All recommendations regarding incidental findings are based on ACR
guidelines for the management of these findings.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  Metastatic renal cell cancer

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT of the chest was obtained from thoracic inlet to upper
abdomen.  Axial images were reviewed in conjunction with coronal and sagittal
reformats

COMPARISON:  ___

FINDINGS: 

Aorta and pulmonary arteries are stable in appearance, unremarkable.  No
mediastinal or axillary lymphadenopathy is present.  No supraclavicular
lymphadenopathy is present.  Bilateral hilar lymph nodes ranging up to 14 mm
are stable.  The enhancement of the right hilar lymph node has slightly
increased compared to previous examination.  Heart size is normal.  Extensive
coronary calcifications are re-demonstrated.  There is no pericardial or
pleural effusion.

Image portion of the upper abdomen will be reviewed separately as part of the
CT abdomen and pelvis in corresponding report will be issued.

Airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally.  Multiple pulmonary
nodules are bilateral, predominantly involving right lung and stable.  No new
nodules masses or consolidations demonstrated.

There are no lytic or sclerotic lesions worrisome for infection or neoplasm.

IMPRESSION: 

No definitive interval progression of intrathoracic metastatic disease.

Bilateral hilar lymph nodes although stable in size demonstrate slight
increase in right hilar enhancement, attention on the follow-up is recommended
to exclude metastatic involvement

Please review CT abdomen and pelvis in the corresponding report for assessment
of the disease progression in the abdomen and pelvis.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:    ___ man with metastatic renal cell carcinoma, restaging
of tumor burden prior to continue treatment.

TECHNIQUE:  Multi detector CT of the chest was performed after the
administration of intravenous contrast.  Axial coronal and sagittal
reconstructions were acquired.  Maximum intensity projections were also
acquired

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 5.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 24.1 mGy (Body) DLP =
12.0 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 8.5 s, 66.6 cm; CTDIvol = 20.7 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,376.6 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 3.8 s, 30.1 cm; CTDIvol = 21.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 635.3
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 2,024 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

COMPARISON:  To a prior study done on ___

FINDINGS: 

THORACIC INLET: The thyroid is unremarkable.  There are no enlarged
supraclavicular lymph nodes.

BREAST AND AXILLA  : There are no enlarged axillary lymph nodes.

MEDIASTINUM: There are no enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes.  There is
moderate coronary artery calcification.  There is no pericardial effusion.The
airways are patent up to the subsegmental level

PLEURA: There is no pleural effusion

LUNG: The esophagus is patulous and dilated.  There is bibasilar atelectasis
and scarring.  A 3 mm nodule in the right middle lobe (3, 89) and a calcified
granuloma in the right upper lobe 3, 88) are unchanged.  A 4 mm right lower
lobe pulmonary nodule 3, 152) is also no new lung nodules are seen.

BONES AND CHEST WALL : Review of bones shows degenerative changes involving
the thoracic spine.

UPPER ABDOMEN: Limited sections through the upper abdomen are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

Stable tiny right lung nodules.  No new lung nodules.  Minimal bibasilar
atelectasis.

Please refer to dedicated report on abdomen which has been dictated separately

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with RCC and cholangiocarcinoma s/p 3 cycles of
gem/cis// assess for disease response

TECHNIQUE:   Multi-detector helical scanning of the chest, coordinated with
intravenous infusion of nonionic, iodinated contrast agent, following oral
administration of contrast agent for selected abdominal studies, and/or
followed by scanning of the neck, was reconstructed as contiguous 5 mm and 1.0
or 1.25 mm thick axial, 2.5 or 5 mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 mm
MIP axial images. Concurrent scanning of the abdomen and pelvis and/or neck
will be reported separately. All images of the chest were reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 3.7 s, 29.1 cm; CTDIvol = 4.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 116.7
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 3.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 14.4 mGy (Body) DLP =
7.2 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 4.2 s, 27.3 cm; CTDIvol = 14.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 402.1
mGy-cm.
   4) Spiral Acquisition 8.6 s, 67.6 cm; CTDIvol = 15.2 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,024.5 mGy-cm.
   5) Spiral Acquisition 3.5 s, 27.1 cm; CTDIvol = 14.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 399.9
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,950 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS W AND W/O CONTRAST, ADDL SECTIONS)

COMPARISON:  Compared to chest CT scans since ___, most recently ___

FINDINGS: 

CHEST PERIMETER: No thyroid findings warrant further imaging.  Supraclavicular
and axillary lymph nodes are not enlarged.  There are no soft tissue
abnormalities in the imaged chest wall concerning for malignancy.  Findings
below the diaphragm will be reported separately.

CARDIO-MEDIASTINUM:Upper esophagus is mildly patulous, but otherwise
unremarkable.

Atherosclerotic calcification is mild in head and neck vessels but heavy in
all major coronary arteries.  Aortic valve is not appreciably calcified. 
Aorta and pulmonary arteries are normal size.  Pericardium is physiologic.



THORACIC LYMPH NODES: As follows:

Mediastinum right lower paratracheal, 9 mm, 6:81, previously 6 mm.

Right posterior paraesophageal mediastinum, 10 x 13 mm, unchanged since at
least ___.


Right hilum, 17 x 17 mm, stable since ___, previously 16 x 14 mm in
___, 18 x 15 mm in ___, 19 x 13 mm in ___.  Small
lymph nodes elsewhere in the right hilus are smaller today than in ___.

Lymph nodes elsewhere in the chest are not pathologically enlarged.

LUNGS, AIRWAYS, PLEURAE: Previous left upper lobe pneumonia has resolved.

No new or growing lung nodules or other focal lung lesions of consequence.

Following nodules are stable:

4-5 mm , right upper lobe, 6:98, stable since ___

6 mm, right middle lobe, 6:164, stable since ___

Punctate nodule right lower lobe, 6:146, stable since ___

2-3 mm, left upper lobe, 06:27, stable since ___


3 mm, right lower lobe, 6:146, stable since ___

4 mm, 3-4 mm, right lower lobe, 6:167, 176, both stable since ___.

Tracheobronchial tree is normal to subsegmental levels.  There are no pleural
abnormalities.
CHEST CAGE: Although there are no bone lesions in the imaged chest cage
suspicious for malignancy or infection, it should be noted that radionuclide
bone and FDG PET scanning are more sensitive in detecting early osseous
pathology than chest CT scanning.

IMPRESSION: 

Slight growth of subcentimeter right lower paratracheal mediastinal node and
fluctuation in size of 17 mm right hilar node over the past 18 months are not
strong evidence of active intrathoracic malignancy, concurrent with a handful
of tiny lung nodules all for at least ___ years.

Severe atherosclerotic coronary calcification.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with RCC and cholangiocarcinoma s/p 6 cycles of
gem/cis// assess for disease response

TECHNIQUE:   Multi-detector helical scanning of the chest, coordinated with
intravenous infusion of nonionic, iodinated contrast agent, following oral
administration of contrast agent for selected abdominal studies, and/or
followed by scanning of the neck, was reconstructed as contiguous 5 mm and 1.0
or 1.25 mm thick axial, 2.5 or 5 mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 mm
MIP axial images. Concurrent scanning of the abdomen and pelvis and/or neck
will be reported separately. All images of the chest were reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 3.5 s, 27.6 cm; CTDIvol = 5.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 156.0
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 3.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 14.4 mGy (Body) DLP =
7.2 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 4.2 s, 27.3 cm; CTDIvol = 21.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 580.9
mGy-cm.
   4) Spiral Acquisition 8.6 s, 68.1 cm; CTDIvol = 20.7 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,410.1 mGy-cm.
   5) Spiral Acquisition 3.4 s, 26.9 cm; CTDIvol = 19.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 535.5
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 2,690 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS W AND W/O CONTRAST, ADDL SECTIONS)

COMPARISON:  Multiple prior chest CTs, most recently ___.

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE AND CHEST WALL:
The thyroid is unremarkable.  No enlarged lymph nodes in either axilla or
thoracic inlet.  Right anterior port with tip in the lower SVC.  No
abnormalities on the chest wall.  Mild atherosclerotic calcifications in the
head and neck arteries.

HEART AND VASCULATURE:
The heart is normal in size and shape.  No pericardial effusion.  Severe sore
sclerotic calcifications in the coronary arteries, mild in the aorta and none
in the cardiac valves.  The pulmonary arteries and aorta are normal in caliber
throughout.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA:
The esophagus is unremarkable.  Stable 9 mm mediastinal lymph node in the
right lower paratracheal station (6:82).  No hilar lymphadenopathy.

PLEURA:
No pleural effusions.  Mild bilateral apical scarring.

LUNGS:
The airways are patent to subsegmental levels.  No bronchial wall thickening,
bronchiectasis or mucus plugging.  Stable 7 mm nodule in the middle lobe (6:
161), 5 mm nodule in the right upper lobe (6:95).  Stable punctate nodule in
the left apex (___).  No new nodules.

CHEST CAGE:
Moderate dorsal spondylosis.  No acute fractures.  No suspicious lytic or
sclerotic lesions.

UPPER ABDOMEN:
Please refer to same day abdominal CT report for subdiaphragmatic findings.

IMPRESSION: 

No interval change compared to prior study of ___.  Stable small lung
nodules and mediastinal lymphadenopathy, likely stable metastatic disease.  No
new or growing lung nodules, lymphadenopathy or osseous lesions.

limited, demonstrates no abnormality.

INDICATION:  History: ___ with pleuritic pain, +D DImer.  Evaluate for PE.

after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast.  Reformatted

subsegmental level without filling defect to indicate a pulmonary embolus. The
thoracic aorta has scattered atherosclerotic calcifications but is normal in
caliber without evidence of dissection or intramural hematoma.  The heart,
pericardium, and great vessels are within normal limits. No pericardial
effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: Lungs are clear without masses or areas of parenchymal
opacification.  Multiple scattered pulmonary nodules are identified including
a 4 mm right lower lobe sub solid nodule (301:87), 2 mm left upper lobe
pulmonary nodule (301:74), 3 mm left lower lobe pulmonary nodule (301:114),
oblong 4 mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule (301:172), subpleural right
middle lobe pulmonary nodule measuring up to 4 mm (301:121), 3 mm right lower
lobe pulmonary nodule (301:113), 2 mm right middle lobe pulmonary nodule
(301:107), a 2 mm right upper lobe pulmonary nodule (301:79), and a 2 mm right
upper lobe pulmonary nodule posteriorly (301:47).  The airways are patent to
the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally.

BASE OF NECK: Visualized portions of the base of the neck show no abnormality.

ABDOMEN:  Included portion of the upper abdomen is unremarkable.

BONES: No suspicious osseous abnormality is seen.? There is no acute fracture.


IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.
2. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules measuring up to 4 mm.  Please see
recommendations below.

smaller than 6mm, no CT follow-up is recommended in a low-risk patient, and an
optional CT follow-up in 12 months is recommended in a high-risk patient.

Nodules Incidentally Detected on CT" for comments and reference:

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  Patient is an ___ female with right hemothorax status
post chest tube placement.  Evaluate for interval change in pleural effusion,
evaluate for a possible lung injury.

EXAMINATION:  CT of the chest with contrast.

COMPARISONS:  Comparison to radiographs from ___ and ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Helically acquired axial images from the thoracic inlet to the mid
abdomen were obtained after administration of 75 mL of Optiray intravenous
contrast.  Timing was performed using a general CT chest protocol.  Coronal
and sagittal reformations were obtained.

FINDINGS:

CT OF THE CHEST WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:

Since radiographs from ___, there has been interval placement of a right-
sided chest tube with tip terminating seen just along the superior aspect of
the right major fissure.  For example, best seen on (41B:17) and is likely
tracking along the fissure, however, intraparenchymal course cannot be
entirely excluded.  There are small areas of right-sided pneumothorax along
the anterior aspect of the right middle lobe adjacent to tracking along the
minor fissure, and with a punctate focus tracking along the major fissure
along the posterior aspect of the right upper lobe.  There is a small amount
of subcutaneous emphysema, likely related to recent chest tube placement.

There is a residual, moderate in size pleural effusion that measures slightly
higher attenuation than simple fluid compatible with known hemothorax.  There
is associated adjacent atelectasis of the right lower lobe with air
bronchograms seen.  In addition, there is a mild amount of linear atelectasis
involving the right middle lobe.  There is a small hazy 4-mm ground-glass
attenuation focus, best seen on (2:10 within the right upper lobe)
representing a 4-mm ground-glass pulmonary nodule.  The right lung is
otherwise clear with no other pulmonary parenchymal opacities or pulmonary
nodules identified.  The left lung is clear with no pulmonary or parenchymal
opacities, pulmonary nodules, or pleural effusions.

There is no hilar, axillary, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.  There are
several scattered pretracheal lymph nodes that do not meet size criteria for
pathologic enlargement.  There is moderate cardiomegaly.  Note is made of
atherosclerotic calcifications within the thoracic aorta.

This examination is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation.  There is
extensive pneumobilia that can be correlated with recent known outside
hospital ERCP procedure performed.  There is stable appearance of a
homogeneously heavily calcified 7 mm granuloma located in the left upper
quadrant.  There are multiple scattered hypodensities seen within the upper
pole of both kidneys that are subcentimeter in size and too small to
accurately characterize, however, statistically, likely represent simple
cysts.  There is a small-sized hiatal hernia.

BONE WINDOWS:  There are known extensive left thoracic rib fractures
particularly involving T6, T7, T8, T9 and T10 with near complete lateral
displacement of the T8 lateral thoracic rib.  There are multilevel
degenerative changes involving the thoracic spine with marginal osteophyte
formation.  There is likely a chronic in nature, vertebral planus compression
deformity of the T12 vertebral body.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Status post right chest tube placement that courses along the right major
fissure with tip seen coursing along the right major fissure.  Small to
moderate-sized right pleural fluid measures high attenuation compatible with
hemothorax.  Small areas of pneumothorax seen tracking along the right minor
fissure and also a punctate focus along the right major fissure.

2.  4-mm ground-glass nodule seen within the right upper lobe for which
recommend clinical correlation, and a dedicated repeat high-resolution chest
CT can be performed within six months for further evaluation if patient of
high risk.

3.  Known extensive thoracic rib fractures as described above and T12 wedge
compression deformity.

4.  Small hiatal hernia.

5.  Extensive pneumobilia, compatible with known recent outside hospital ERCP.

the right major fissure.  For example, best seen on (41B:17) and is likely
tracking along the fissure, however, intraparenchymal course cannot be
entirely excluded.  There are small areas of right-sided pneumothorax along
the anterior aspect of the right middle lobe adjacent to tracking along the
minor fissure, and with a punctate focus tracking along the major fissure
along the posterior aspect of the right upper lobe.  There is a small amount
of subcutaneous emphysema, likely related to recent chest tube placement.

There is a residual, moderate in size pleural effusion that measures slightly
higher attenuation than simple fluid compatible with known hemothorax.  There
is associated adjacent atelectasis of the right lower lobe with air
bronchograms seen.  In addition, there is a mild amount of linear atelectasis
involving the right middle lobe.  There is a small hazy 4-mm ground-glass
attenuation focus, best seen on (2:10 within the right upper lobe)
representing a 4-mm ground-glass pulmonary nodule.  The right lung is
otherwise clear with no other pulmonary parenchymal opacities or pulmonary
nodules identified.  The left lung is clear with no pulmonary or parenchymal
opacities, pulmonary nodules, or pleural effusions.

There is no hilar, axillary, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.  There are
several scattered pretracheal lymph nodes that do not meet size criteria for
pathologic enlargement.  There is moderate cardiomegaly.  Note is made of
atherosclerotic calcifications within the thoracic aorta.

This examination is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation.  There is
extensive pneumobilia that can be correlated with recent known outside
hospital ERCP procedure performed.  There is stable appearance of a
homogeneously heavily calcified 7 mm granuloma located in the left upper
quadrant.  There are multiple scattered hypodensities seen within the upper
pole of both kidneys that are subcentimeter in size and too small to
accurately characterize, however, statistically, likely represent simple
cysts.  There is a small-sized hiatal hernia.

BONE WINDOWS:  There are known extensive left thoracic rib fractures
particularly involving T6, T7, T8, T9 and T10 with near complete lateral
displacement of the T8 lateral thoracic rib.  There are multilevel
degenerative changes involving the thoracic spine with marginal osteophyte
formation.  There is likely a chronic in nature, vertebral planus compression

abdomen are otherwise grossly unremarkable.

CT CHEST.

REASON FOR EXAM:  HCC.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector CT through the chest was acquired after
administration of IV contrast.  Images were displayed in axial, coronal and
sagittal reformations.

FINDINGS:  The airways are patent to the subsegmental levels.  There are no
enlarged axillary lymph nodes.  Prevascular lymph node measures 7 mm.  There
are minimal calcifications in the LAD and circumflex coronary arteries.  There
is mild cardiomegaly.  The aorta is normal in caliber.  There is no pleural or
pericardial effusion.  An epicardial lymph node measures 8 mm.

Please refer to the concurrent CT abdomen for detailed description of the
abdominal findings.

There is a faint sclerotic lesion at T5.

There are a few scattered calcified granulomas.  2 mm nodules are present in
the left upper lobe (7, 28, 87), in the left lower lobe (7, 165).

There are ill-defined ground-glass peribronchial opacities in the left upper
lobe.

IMPRESSION:  Evidence of prior granulomatous infection.

2 mm lung nodules could represent non-calcified granulomas but metastasis
cannot be excluded.  Followup in three months is recommended.

Ground-glass opacities in the left upper lobe are consistent with infectious
process/ aspiration.

Coronary calcification.

Indeterminate sclerotic lesion in T5.  Bone scan is recommended.

radiology_facts.report_text:
REASON FOR EXAMINATION:  Evaluation of the patient with acute leukemia and
breast mass.

COMPARISON:  No prior studies available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT of the chest was obtained from thoracic inlet to upper
abdomen with subsequent 1.25- and 5-mm collimation axial images reviewed in
conjunction with coronal and sagittal reformats.

FINDINGS:

Assessment of the chest reveals left breast nodule, solid and
well-circumscribed, approximately 15.1 x 12.8 mm in diameter, 2:34,
corresponding to the nodule demonstrated on the mammography.  No axillary
pathologically enlarged lymph nodes demonstrated, although several small lymph
nodes are present.  No other nodules within the breast demonstrated.  Neither
ipsi- nor contralaterally.  No hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy is seen. 
Aberrant right subclavian artery is demonstrated, anatomical variant.  Cluster
of lymph nodes is noted at the lower aspect of the right axilla, again not
pathologically enlarged but multiple.

Aorta and pulmonary arteries are unremarkable.  No pericardial or pleural
effusion is seen.  The imaged portion of the upper abdomen reveals no
appreciable abnormality.

For the assessment of the thoracic inlet review CT neck and the corresponding
report.

There are no bone lesions worrisome for infection or neoplasm.

Airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. Assessment of the
lung parenchyma demonstrates several pulmonary nodules:  4:70, 4:136,
4:60,lingular nodule, 4:212.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Left breast nodule as described that should be further correlated with
tissue biopsy.
2.  Right axillary lymph nodes, not pathologically enlarged but multiple.
3.  Several pulmonary nodules that should be reassessed in three months for
assessment of stability.

radiology_facts.report_text:
HISTORY:  ___ man with AML and ongoing neutropenic fever associated
with cough despite broad-spectrum antibiotics.

COMPARISON:  Comparison is made to CT of the chest from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT images are obtained of the thorax without the use of
intravenous contrast.  Reformatted coronal, sagittal and axial maximum
intensity projection images were reviewed.

CT THORAX WITHOUT IV CONTRAST:  Numerous bilateral axillary lymph nodes are
not pathologically enlarged, and are stable to decreased in size since the
prior study (3:14).  No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is present.  Multiple
prominent mediastinal lymph nodes are not  pathologically enlarged, the
measuring up to 6 mm in short axis diameter in the precarinal station (3:13). 
No pleural or pericardial effusion is identified.  The heart and pericardium
are unremarkable.  The intrathoracic aorta and pulmonary arteries are normal
in caliber.

A left breast nodule is again seen (5:154) and intervally decreased in size
since the prior study, now measuring 13 x 11 mm, previously 15 x 13 mm,
consistent with a hematoma, as suggested on diagnostic mammogram and
ultrasound from ___.

Lung windows demonstrate interval development of numerous bilateral semisolid
(5:91) and ground-glass nodules  (5:57), which are randomly distributed in all
lobes of the lungs and range from 1 mm to 3 mm in size (5:134).  Subsegmental
bibasilar atelectasis is also present.  A punctate calcified granuloma is
identified in the right lower lobe (5:171).   A trace left pleural effusion is
present (5:206).

Although the study is not designed for the evaluation of subdiaphragmatic
structures, the unenhanced appearance of the upper abdomen is unremarkable.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  No lytic or blastic lesion suspicious for malignancy is
identified.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Numerous small ground glass and semi-solid pulmonary nodules are randomly
distributed throughout all lobes of both lungs and are new since the prior
study.  Although this finding is not concerning for malignant disease, their
presence in a neutropenic patient could represent an inflammatory or
infectious process such as viral pneumonia.  No evidence of bacterial or
fungal pneumonia.
2.  Interval decrease in size of left breast nodule, in keeping with a
hematoma as described on recent diagnostic mammogram from ___.
3.  Numerous bilateral axillary and mediastinal lymph nodes are not
pathologically enlarged.
4.  Trace left pleural effusion (5:206).

The above findings were communicated to Dr. ___ by Dr. ___ telephone
at 4:15 p.m., within five minutes of discovery.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  History of AML, neutropenic fevers, awaiting allotransplant. 
Pleuritic chest pain.

COMPARISONS:  Chest CT from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial imaging was obtained through the chest following the
administration of intravenous contrast material.  Coronal and sagittal
reformats were completed.

FINDINGS:  The thyroid gland is unremarkable.  There are no enlarged
supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes.  There is a small
new pericardial effusion, simple in nature.  A right chest wall port catheter
tip terminates in the right atrium.  There is no fluid collection around the
catheter course.  The airways are patent to the subsegmental levels.

There is a large area of consolidation within the right upper lobe abutting
the fissure, 4:136, with surrounding ground glass opacities.  Multiple
additional pulmonary opacities, for example in the anterior right upper lobe,
4:102; superior segment of the left upper lobe, 4:134, as well as a focal area
of consolidation in the right lower lobe measuring 2.1 x 2.1 cm (4:95) are
most consistent with multifocal infection.  A 9 x 9 mm nodule in the right
apex, 4:60, is also likely part of the same process and has ground glass
opacities surrounding it.  There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax. 
Bibasilar atelectasis is noted.

This study is not tailored for evaluation of subdiaphragmatic structures. 
Limited views are unremarkable.

There are no suspicious bony lesions.

IMPRESSION:  
1.  Multifocal pulmonary opacities, the largest of which is in the right upper
lobe, with surrounding ground glass opacities concerning for fungal infection
such as invasive aspergillosis.  

2.  Small pericardial effusion, new since the prior exam.

These findings were discussed with Dr. ___ by Dr. ___
telephone at 1:30 p.m.

aberrant retroesophageal right subclavian artery.   Although the study is not
tailored for evaluation of the subdiaphragmatic regions, limited evaluation of
the abdomen is unremarkable.

diffuse bilateral subpleural and peribronchovascular opacities, some in a

peripherally, although also seen in the upper lobes. Additionally, there are
more confluent areas of opacity, particularly at the left lung base and at the

pleural effusion.

the periphery of the lungs, which may be seen with multifocal infection, such
as a viral infection. In the setting of allogeneic bone marrow transplant and
minimal patient symptoms, cryptogenic organizing pneumonia, a diagnosis of
exclusion, may be most likely in this situation.

transplant ___.  With abnormal CT chest on ___, on voriconazole since;

DOSE:  Total DLP = 405.72mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  ___ and ___.

FINDINGS: 

The thyroid gland is unremarkable. Multiple mildly prominent mediastinal lymph
nodes measuring up to 5 mm in short axis are stable.  There is no
supraclavicular, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy.

Heart size is normal with no pericardial effusion. The main pulmonary artery

The majority of the previously seen bilateral upper lobe peripheral opacities
have improved or completely resolved since ___. Some of the more
discrete nodular opacities in the bilateral lower lobes have also resolved.
However, basal and peripheral predominant opacities now have a more
peribronchovascular and perilobular distribution (4, 203 - 205). There is also
new mild traction bronchiectasis ,which is most pronounced in the right middle
lobe and lingula.  Anterior right upper lobe subsegmental atelectasis, volume
loss, bronchiolectasis and scarring is unchanged. There is no pleural
effusion.

The patient is status post left mastectomy.

IMPRESSION: 

Interval improvement in bilateral upper lobe opacities since ___
suggests resolving fungal infection.

However, peripheral and basal predominant opacities now have a more
peribronchovascular and perilobular distribution, with associated bronchial
dilation and distortion.  This pattern is characteristic of organizing
pneumonia.

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with COP s.p allotransplant. Need urgent CT chest
as pt has worsening SOB.  // ___ year old man with COP s.p allotransplant. Need
urgent CT chest as pt has worsening SOB.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was performed without
intravenous contrast agent reconstructed as contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick
axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images.

DOSE:  DLP: 308.0 mGy cm

COMPARISON:  Several prior chest CT examinations dating from ___
through ___.

FINDINGS: 

The thyroid is unremarkable.

Heart size is normal without significant pericardial fluid. The thoracic
aortic arch and main pulmonary artery are normal in caliber. Scattered mildly
prominent mediastinal lymph nodes measure up to 8 mm in the short axis,
unchanged from prior study and on enlarged by CT size criteria. There is no
supraclavicular, axillary, hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy by CT size
criteria.

There is trace biapical scarring. There has been diffuse worsening of
bilateral lower zone predominant peripheral peribronchial and
peribronchovascular ground-glass opacities and areas of consolidation. There
is re- demonstration of diffuse bronchiectasis, surrounding scarring and
associated volume loss. Airways appear to remain patent to the subsegmental
level.

3 mm subpleural ground-glass nodule in the posterior segment of the right
upper lobe is stable (5:92). 2 mm nodule in the anterior segment of the right
upper lobe is unchanged (5:90). Another 2 mm nodule in the anterior segment of
the right upper lobe is stable (5:98). Punctate granuloma is present in the
lateral basal segment of the right lower lobe. Other scattered millimetric
nodules are unchanged. There is no new large consolidation. There is no
pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

While this study is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic diagnosis, the imaged
upper abdomen is notable for diverticulosis.

Osseous structures and soft tissues: There is no suspicious focal osseous
lesion. There are mild multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.
Re- demonstration of postsurgical change from left mastectomy.

IMPRESSION:


1. Mild interval progression of diffuse lower zone and peripheral predominant
peribronchial and peribronchovascular ground-glass opacities since ___.  Features remain suggestive of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia.
2. Stable areas of right middle lobe and lingular predominant scarring,
traction bronchiectasis and volume loss.
3. Stable scattered sub 4 mm nodules.
4. Diverticulosis.

respiratory infection (cough/SOB). Need CT to eval pulmonary status/infection

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was performed without

axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images.

COMPARISON:  ___

FINDINGS: 

The thyroid is normal. Supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal and hilar lymph
nodes are not enlarged. Aorta and pulmonary arteries are normal size. Cardiac
configuration is normal and there is no appreciable coronary calcification.
There is no pleural or pericardial effusion.  Small hiatal hernia.

Mild diffuse bronchial wall thickening and irregularity has increased since
the prior examination.  Although an expiratory scan was not obtained, relative
mosaic attenuation of the lungs, most pronounced in the lower lobes is
suggestive of air trapping.

Multiple pulmonary nodules are unchanged from ___.  For example:

A 2 mm nodule in the left upper lobe (series 4, image 56).
A 3 mm subpleural nodule in the right upper lobe (series 4, image 62).
A 2 mm nodule in the right upper lobe (series 4, image 75).
A 5 mm ground-glass nodule in the right upper lobe (4:69)
A 4 mm subpleural ground-glass nodule in the right lower lobe (series 4, image
274).

No new nodules are identified.  Granulomas are incidentally noted in the right
lower lobe and the lingula.

This examination is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation, however the
intra-abdominal organs appear grossly normal.

There are no bone findings of malignancy.

Patient is status post left mastectomy.  Mild right gynecomastia is unchanged.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Mild diffuse bronchial wall thickening has increased since the prior
examination.  This could potentially reflect on acute bronchitis in the
appropriate clinical setting, versus progressive chronic graft-versus-host
disease.

2. Numerous pulmonary nodules are stable in size and morphology.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:      AML

TECHNIQUE:  MULTI DETECTOR HELICAL SCANNING OF THE CHEST WAS COORDINATED WITH
INTRAVENOUS INFUSION OF NONIONIC CONTRAST AGENT, RECONSTRUCTED AS CONTIGUOUS 5
AND 1.25 MM THICK AXIAL, 5 MM THICK CORONAL AND PARASAGITTAL, AND 8 MM MIP
AXIAL IMAGES. SUBSEQUENT SCANNING OF THE ABDOMEN AND PELVIS AND THE TOTAL
DOSAGE OF SCANNING THE ENTIRE TORSO WILL BE REPORTED SEPARATELY.

DOSAGE:  TOTAL DLP reported separatelymGy-cm

COMPARISON:  ___.

FINDINGS: 

Small, subcentimeter axillary and mediastinal lymph nodes are similar in size
and number to the prior CT.  Heart size is normal, and there is no pericardial
effusion.  Small bilateral pleural effusions are new, dependent in location,
with adjacent bibasilar atelectasis.  Additional foci of linear atelectasis
are present in the mid lungs bilaterally.

Skeletal structures of the thorax demonstrate no new suspicious lytic or
blastic lesions.

Within the lungs, assessment is limited by inadvertent expiratory phase of
respiration and motion artifact.  Diffuse ground-glass opacities and septal
thickening are new since the prior CT. Previously reported subcentimeter
nodules are difficult to compare to the prior study due to the technical
limitations described above.  A 9 mm sub solid nodule in the left upper lobe
(97, 6) is potentially slightly more dense and larger than on the prior study,
but technical differences limit comparison.  Additionally, a few of the
previously described opacities are potentially obscured by new dependent
atelectasis.



IMPRESSION: 

1.  New ground-glass opacities with septal thickening and dependent small
pleural effusions, most suggestive of hydrostatic edema.  Differential
diagnosis includes atypical infection and less likely leukemic infiltration.

2. Pre-existing lung nodules are difficult to compare to the prior CT due to
technical limitations of today's exam.  Consider a ___ month followup CT to
allow more precise comparison of a potentially growing left upper lobe nodule
in order to exclude the possibility of a slowly growing lesion within the lung
adenocarcinoma spectrum.

3.  Please see separately dictated CT of the abdomen and pelvis for complete
description of subdiaphragmatic findings.

infection.

The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber.  There is an aberrant right

indicate the presence of any acute incidental central pulmonary embolus.

physiologic.

criteria.  Scattered mediastinal lymph nodes are measurable up to 6 mm in

The imaged thyroid is unremarkable.

No osseous lesions in the chest cage concerning for malignancy or infection. 
Multi-level degenerative changes in the thoracic spine are mild.  No acute
fracture.  There is asymmetry of the breast, suggesting prior excision on the
left.  There is right asymmetric gynecomastia.

This exam is not dedicated for imaging of the upper abdomen, but the partially

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast.

INDICATION:  History: ___ with s/p fall with significant chest pain and
tenderness  // ?rib fractures ?pneumothorax

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images were acquired through the chest, abdomen and
pelvis following intravenous contrast administration with split bolus
technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Total DLP (Body) = 508 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen and pelvis dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

CHEST:
HEART AND VASCULATURE:  Atherosclerotic calcifications noted of the aortic
arch, at the origin of the head and neck vessels, and of the coronary
arteries.  Mild calcifications of the aortic annulus.  The thoracic aorta is
normal in caliber without evidence of acute injury.  The heart, pericardium,
and great vessels are within normal limits. No pericardial effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM:  Multiple enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes are
demonstrated.  Right peritracheal measuring 12 x 11 mm (series 2, image 17). 
Left peritracheal lymph node conglomerate measuring 2.9 x 1.5 cm (series 2,
image 28).  Right precarinal measuring 10 x 8 mm.  (series 2, image 30).  Left
hilus measuring 1.5 x 1.0 cm (series 2, image 41).  No axillary
lymphadenopathy.  No mediastinal mass or hematoma.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS:  Partially calcified pleural parenchymal scarring is seen
within the right upper lobe.  Mild dependent atelectasis bilaterally.  No
focal consolidations.  5 mm solid nodular opacity within the left lower lobe
(series 2, image 39) likely represents atelectasis.  The airways are patent to
the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally.

BASE OF NECK: Visualized portions of the base of the neck show no abnormality.

ABDOMEN:
HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates slightly heterogeneous attenuation
without evidence of focal lesions.  There is no evidence of laceration.  There
is no evidence of intrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is
surgically absent.  The common bile duct is prominent, likely due to
postcholecystectomy state.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions.  Mild pancreatic ductal dilatation, unchanged, likely due to
prior pancreatitis.  There is no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesion or laceration.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: There are numerous hypodensities within the kidneys bilaterally, some
of which are consistent with simple cysts, others are too small to
characterize.  Otherwise, the kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with
normal nephrogram.  There is no evidence of solid renal lesions or
hydronephrosis.  Mild perinephric stranding bilaterally is within normal
limits for patient of this age.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  There is moderate
wall thickening and mild fat stranding surrounding an approximately 9 cm
segment of proximal transverse colon (series 2, image 121), likely
representing segmental colitis.  There is no nodularity to the colonic wall
thickening to suggest a neoplasm.  Portions of the ascending and descending
colon are collapsed.  The large bowel enhances normally.  The appendix is
normal.  There is no evidence of mesenteric injury.

There is no free fluid or free air in the abdomen.

PELVIS: The bladder is decompressed and cannot be adequately evaluated on this
examination.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: There is a large gastrohepatic lymph node conglomerate with
cystic components measuring 2.7 x 1.4 cm (series 2, images 94).  There is an
additional large portacaval lymph node conglomerate measuring 3.5 x 2.0 cm. 
There are multiple subcentimeter periaortic retroperitoneal lymph nodes, the
largest at the level of the renal veins measuring 11 x 7 mm.  There is no
pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm or retroperitoneal hematoma. 
Moderate atherosclerotic disease is noted.

BONES: Grade 1 anterolisthesis of L4 on L5, unchanged.  There is no acute
fracture.  Multiple chronic anterior rib fractures are seen. No focal
suspicious osseous abnormality.

SOFT TISSUES: Tiny fat containing umbilical hernia.  Injection granulomas are
seen overlying the gluteal muscles bilaterally.  Otherwise, the abdominal and
pelvic wall is within normal limits.


IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of traumatic injury within the chest, abdomen or pelvis.
2. Numerous enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes and gastrohepatic and portacaval
lymph node conglomerate measuring up to 3.5 x 2.0 cm with possibly cystic
components, suspicious for malignancy, although a definite primary is not
visualized on this examination.  Lymphoma is a consideration.
3. Focal segment of proximal transverse colon demonstrating wall thickening
and surrounding fat stranding, which likely represents segmental colitis.  No
nodularity to suggest an underlying primary malignancy.
4. Grade 1 anterolisthesis of L4 on L5, unchanged.

axial images.  Contrast agent was not administered. All images were reviewed.

radiology_facts.report_text:
PROCEDURE:  CT chest without contrast on ___.

COMPARISON:  None similar are available.

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images were obtained from the thoracic inlet to
the subdiaphragmatic area without contrast.  Thinner slice 5 mm and 1.25 mm
images were reconstructed in the axial plane at different window algorithms.
Sagittal/coronal reformatted images were also obtained for further evaluation.

HISTORY:  ___ man with new diagnosis of left non-small cell lung
cancer here for evaluation.  Evaluate pleural space and airways.

FINDINGS:  7 x 9 x 10-cm lobulated heterogeneous soft tissue mass with
multiple areas of low attenuation likely necrosis are seen involving the left
lung with endoluminal involvement of the left main bronchus before its
bifurcation causing almost complete collapse of the left lung and leftward
shift of the cardiomediastinal structures.  The non-collapsed part of the left
lung base demonstrates peribronchovesicular thickening.  The adjacent pleural
space is invaded with presence of a loculated pleural effusion superiorly and
multiple soft tissue nodularity.  The effusion is mainly confined to the left
upper hemithorax lateral compartment.  There is a left chest drainage catheter
introduced from the left chest wall close to the base with the tip terminating
at the level of the carina.

Subpleural lucencies are seen along the right margin of the right upper lobe
indicative of mild paraseptal emphysema.  A clustered area of lucency is seen
in the right upper lobe adjacent to the fissure with adjacent ground-glass
opacity and another area is noted in the right lower lobe likely
pneumatoceles, sequelae of prior infection.

A solid nodule in the right lobe of the lung (3:45) measures 4.5 mm.

There is no right pleural effusion.  There is minimal pericardial effusion
confined to the left pericardial space with associated nodularity and
infiltration of the mediastinal fat.  Extensive atherosclerotic calcifications
are seen involving the origin of the major vessels including the
brachiocephalic trunk, the carotid and the left subclavian artery, in
addition, extensive atherosclerotic calcifications are seen involving the
coronary arteries both right and left, left more than right.  A focus of
calcification is seen involving the chorda tendineae of the left ventricle, a
sequela of prior old myocardial infarction.

Within the mediastinum, mainly to the left are multiple non-fat-containing
pathologically enlarged lymph nodes; 2 cm left prevascular lymph node, 1.4 cm
left prevascular lymph node, 1.5 cm subcarinal lymph node, 8 mm right hilar
lymph node.

The bony structures do not show any lesions suspicious for malignancy and/or
infection.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ man with progressive altered mental status and
atypical cells and elevated protein in the CSF, suspicious for a
paraneoplastic syndrome.  Evaluate for lymphadenopathy or other signs of
malignancy.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
intravenous infusion of nonionic iodinated contrast agent reconstructed as
contiguous 5- and 1.25-mm thick axial, 2.5-mm thick coronal and parasagittal,
and 8 x 8 mm MIPs axial images.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Sequenced Acquisition 0.5 s, 0.2 cm; CTDIvol = 9.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.9
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 1.8 s, 0.2 cm; CTDIvol = 30.3 mGy (Body) DLP =
6.1 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 11.5 s, 74.9 cm; CTDIvol = 8.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 662.4
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 670 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph of ___.

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE: The imaged thyroid is normal. Supraclavicular
and axillary lymph nodes are not enlarged by CT size criteria.

MEDIASTINUM:  There are homogeneously enhancing left paratracheal and
subcarinal lymph nodes measuring up to 9 mm in short axis.

HILA: Sub cm short axis left hilar lymph nodes are present.

HEART and PERICARDIUM: The aorta and main pulmonary artery are normal in size.
Limited evaluation for pulmonary embolism due to inadequate contrast
opacification of the segmental and subsegmental pulmonary arteries.  No large
filling defect noted in the main or lobar pulmonary arteries on both sides.
Heart size is normal with severe diffuse native coronary artery
calcifications.  Calcification at the left ventricular apex likely represents
sequela of old infarction.  There is no pericardial effusion.

PLEURA: There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS:  In the superior segment of the left lower lobe, there is a 3.4
x 2.5 x 3.2 cm mass with irregular margins (4:153, 8:52).  No overt retraction
of the adjacent major fissure.  In the anterior segment of the right upper
lobe, there is dense consolidation with a feeding vessel, spanning
approximately 3.2 cm (4:114), with adjacent ground-glass opacity and mild
architectural distortion of the lung.  A 3 mm nodule is identified in the left
upper lobe (4:75).

UPPER ABDOMEN:  Please refer to the dedicated CT abdomen and pelvis report of
the same date for the subdiaphragmatic findings.

CHEST CAGE/BONES:  No focal lytic or sclerotic lesion concerning for
malignancy. Mild to moderate multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracic
spine are present.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  3.4 x 2.5 x 3.2 cm mass in the superior segment of the left lower lobe,
concerning for a primary lung neoplasm.  If clinically indicated, this would
likely be amenable to bronchoscopic approach for tissue sampling. 
Additionally, PET-CT could be considered.

2.  Dense consolidation in the right upper lobe spans approximately 3.2 cm
with adjacent ground-glass opacity and mild parenchymal architectural
distortion.  While this could be infectious/ inflammatory in etiology,
additional neoplastic involvement is considered.

3.  Please refer to the dedicated CT abdomen pelvis report of the same date
for the subdiaphragmatic findings.

NOTIFICATION:  The above findings and recommendation were communicated via
telephone by Dr. ___ to Dr. ___ at 21:03 on ___, 2 min
after discovery.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  Patient with diabetes and positive blood cultures.  Waxing and
waning chest pain.  Chest x-ray findings concerning for postobstructive
atelectasis.  Please assess.

COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph of the same day.

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images through the chest were obtained without IV
contrast.  Coronal and sagittal reformatted images were generated.

CT OF THE CHEST WITHOUT CONTRAST:  There is a large oval opacity in the
anterior segment of the right upper lobe measuring 5.6 x 2.8 cm in the axial
___ (3:28).  There is opacification/an abrupt cutoff of the medial
branch of the distal anterior segment bronchus of the right upper lobe
(4:104), located immediately proximal to the large oval opacity of the
anterior segment.  The findings are highly suspicious for an endobronchial
lesion or possibly impaction resulting in post- obstructive disease. A mass
comprising the entire opacity would be difficult to exclude in the absence of
IV contrast.

Several additional nodular opacities are seen within the lungs, including a 12
x 8 mm nodule at the right lung apex (4:33), a 7-mm mixed soft tissue/ground
glass in the left upper lobe (4:53), a 4-mm nodule of the posterior right
upper lobe (4:101), a peripheral left upper lobe opacity measuring 6 mm
(4:111), and 3 and 4-mm nodules of the lingula and left lower lobe,
respectively (4:123).  There is patchy dependent atelectasis in both lungs.

No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymph nodes meet CT size criteria for
pathologic enlargement.  There is no pleural or pericardial effusion.  There
are coronary artery calcifications involving the LAD and left circumflex.

The exam was not tailored for subdiaphragmatic diagnosis, but the imaged
portion of the liver, spleen, pancreas, and right adrenal gland are
unremarkable.  There is an indeterminate soft tissue nodule that is at the
posterior aspect of the left adrenal gland measuring 1.8 x 1.4 cm, not fully
characterized.  Contrast continues to be excreted from the imaged kidneys
related to CT examination last night.

There are no suspicious osteolytic or sclerotic lesions.

IMPRESSION:

1.  There is strong concern for an endobronchial lesion or impaction of the
medial branch of the distal RUL anterior segment bronchus.  A 5.6 x 2.8- cm
opacity distal to this obstruction may represent post- obstructive
atelectasis, post-obstructive pneumonia, or mass lesion.  These findings would
be better characterized with IV contrast administration. Furthermore,
bronchoscopy may be quite helpful in the evaluation.

2.  There are multiple additional nodular opacities in both lungs.  A sizable
nodule is located at the right lung apex and a mixed ground glass and solid
nodule is in the left upper lobe, and these two lesions specifically are
worrisome for neoplasm.  Consider a PET CT for further assessment.

3.  Coronary artery calcifications.

4.  Indeterminate nodule posterior to the left adrenal, as described on recent
abdominal CT.  Consider a dedicated adrenal CT or MRI for characterization.

Findings and recommendations were discussed with Dr. ___ on the evening of
___.

EXAMINATION:  CTA CHEST WANDW/O CANDRECONS, NON-CORONARY

INDICATION:  History: ___ with resp failure, unresponsive.  recent cr 0.53  //
PE?

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial multidetector CT images through the chest during
rapid administration of intravenous contrast as per ___ PE protocol.
Multiplanar reformations.

Total DLP: 654 mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  None

FINDINGS: 

CTA Chest: The pulmonary arteries are well opacified to the subsegmental level
and there is no filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism. The aortic arch
and LAD predominant coronary artery calcifications are noted. There is no
evidence of aortic dissection.  The ascending aorta is mildly dilated to 4 cm.

CT Chest: Thyroid enhances homogeneously. Axillary, mediastinal, and hilar
lymph nodes do not meet CT size criteria for lymphadenopathy. Heart is normal
size. There is no pericardial effusion.

Endotracheal tube terminates approximately 7 cm above subcarinal node should
be advanced by 2 cm for more optimal placement.  The airways are patent to
subsegmental level and notable for moderate bronchiectasis with lower lobe
predominance. There is also moderate centrilobular emphysema. A cluster of
multiple solid pulmonary nodule in an area of parenchymal distortion is
present in the right upper lobe, the largest measuring 11 x 6 mm (3:54). Two
left upper lobe pulmonary nodules measure up to 8 x 6 mm (3:81, 03:26).
Bibasilar opacities likely reflect atelectasis.  There is bilateral lower lobe
bronchiectasis.  1There is no pleural effusion. Pleural calcifications are
present along the posterior lower lobes.

Limited view of the upper abdomen is notable for a stone filled gallbladder.
NG tube is within the stomach.

No concerning lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion is present.  Age indeterminate
compression deformities are present in T3, T6, T7, T9, and T12 without
evidence of retropulsion.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Endotracheal tube terminates approximately 7 cm above subcarinal node
should be advanced by 2 cm for more optimal placement.

2.  No evidence of pulmonary embolism.

3.  Mildly dilated ascending aorta to 4 cm.

4.  Multiple solid pulmonary nodules warrant followup in 3 months to assess
for stability or resolution.

5.  Age-indeterminate compression deformities in T3, T6, T7, T9, and T12
without evidence of retropulsion.

6.  Cholelithiasis, gallbladder full of stones.

radiology_facts.report_text:
INDICATION:  ___ PMH HTN, HFpEF, IDDM, ESRD due to DM s/p LURT ___
(livingdonor: husband; maintained on MMF and tacrolimus), pericardialeffusion
(___), 3 recent admissions for CHF ___, ___,
___, presents with ___// New lower right lung opacity on CXR
(Aspiration Vs edema Vs atelectasis)

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images were obtained through the chest without
intravenous contrast.  Coronal and sagittal reformats were obtained.

COMPARISON:  Correlation made to CT abdomen from ___.

FINDINGS: 

HEART AND VASCULATURE:  The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber.  There are
atherosclerotic calcifications including coronary artery calcifications and
valvular calcifications.  Heart size is grossly normal.  Again visualized is
patient's known moderate to large pericardial effusion.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: There are enlarged right paratracheal lymph
nodes measuring up to 2.2 x 1.9 cm.  Enlarged subcarinal lymph node measuring
4.0 x 2.7 x 1.9 cm.  Multiple enlarged prevascular lymph nodes measuring up to
13 mm.  Probable right hilar lymphadenopathy however limited due to lack of
contrast.

PLEURAL SPACES: Stable small right pleural effusion and trace left pleural
effusion.  No pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: Masslike consolidation involving the suprahilar right upper
lobe measuring 4.0 x 4.1 x 3.7 cm.  There are patchy nodular airspace
opacities distally within the right upper lobe.  Less significant nodular
airspace opacities involving the remaining lobes.  Material seen occluding the
right upper lobe bronchus.  Airways are otherwise patent.

BASE OF NECK: There is a partially calcified nodule arising from the lower
pole left lobe of the thyroid measuring 2.9 x 2.3 x 2.8 cm.  Soft tissues of
the visualized neck are otherwise unremarkable.

ABDOMEN: No significant change from prior CT abdomen including adrenal nodular
thickening and renal atrophy.

BONES: No suspicious osseous abnormality is seen.? There is no acute fracture.


IMPRESSION: 

Right suprahilar masslike consolidation with occlusion of the right upper lobe
bronchus.  Although this could represent bronchopneumonia in the setting of
mucous plugging appearance raises suspicion for primary lung malignancy. 
There is nodular airspace disease noted distally within the right upper lobe
suggestive of a postobstructive pneumonia/pneumonitis.  Recommend pulmonology
consultation and bronchoscopic correlation.

Mediastinal and probable right hilar lymphadenopathy as detailed above could
be reactive to infectious/inflammatory process or could represent metastatic
disease.

Redemonstration of known moderate to large pericardial effusion, small right
pleural effusion, and trace left pleural effusion.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  pulmonology consultation and bronchoscopic correlation, as
above

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with obstructive R upper lobe mass (___ lung
CA), worsening CXR and respiratory status despite fluid removal// Evaluate for

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 2.3 s, 36.7 cm; CTDIvol = 19.7 mGy (Body) DLP = 722.2
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 722 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  Comparison is made prior chest radiograph done ___
at 08:13, prior CT chest done ___ and prior CT abdomen done ___

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: Partially calcified 24 mm left lobe
of thyroid nodule is again noted.  Mildly enlarged right supraclavicular lymph
nodes appear fairly similar compared to prior.  Irregular soft tissue
thickening involving the upper anterior abdominal wall with associated
calcification (series 2, image 52 and image 58) are again noted.

UPPER ABDOMEN: Atrophic appearance of the native kidneys.  Incompletely imaged
lesion in relation to the lower pole of the left kidney is again noted and
reference is made to prior CT abdomen report of ___. 
Cholelithiasis.  Nodular appearance of the adrenals are unchanged

MEDIASTINUM: Extensive mediastinal adenopathy for example right lower
paratracheal lymph node measuring 17 mm in diameter (series 2, image 16) as
well as subcarinal lymphadenopathy measuring 27 mm in AP diameter (series 2,
image 22) appears fairly similar compared to prior imaging.

HILA: Right hilar adenopathy is difficult to delineate without contrast but
appears fairly similar compared to prior the.

HEART and PERICARDIUM: Relative hypodensity of the blood pool suggesting
anemia.  Severe coronary artery calcification noted.  Moderate sized
pericardial effusion measuring up to 30 mm in diameter adjacent to the left
ventricle appears similar compared to prior.

PLEURA: Right-sided chest drain in situ.  Interval decrease in size of the
previously noted right-sided pleural effusion.  There is also interval
improvement in the previously noted right lower lobe atelectasis.  Small
residual right anterosuperior pneumothorax, which can be anticipated with
presence of in situ drain.

LUNG:

1. PARENCHYMA:  The previously noted right upper lobe obstructing mass is
again noted completely occluding the anterior segmental bronchus of the right
upper lobe with associated obstructive pneumonitis and atelectasis in the
anterior segment of the right upper lobe distal to this.  The airspace
opacification has increased compared to prior.  Peribronchial opacities in the
right lower lobe and to a lesser degree in the rest of the lungs are
new/increased compared to prior concerning for bronchopneumonia/aspiration. 
Atelectasis in the left lung base has increased substantially.  Small
associated left-sided pleural effusion.  Interstitial thickening in the lung
apices and lung bases are concerning for interstitial pulmonary edema.
2. AIRWAYS: Complete obstruction of the anterior segmental bronchus of the
right upper lobe as described above.
3. VESSELS:  The pulmonary artery is dilated measuring up to 36 mm in diameter
suggesting pulmonary hypertension.  Extensive arterial vascular
calcifications.
CHEST CAGE: Degenerative changes.  No lytic/destructive bony lesions.

IMPRESSION: 

Again noted is a right central right upper lobe mass with postobstructive
pneumopathy.  Degree of postobstructive atelectasis of the anterior segment of
the right upper lobe appears increased since the prior chest CT.  Extensive
mediastinal lymphadenopathy as well as right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
are unchanged.

Interval placement of a chest tube with interval decrease in size of the
previously noted right pleural effusion and right base atelectasis.  There is
extensive peribronchial nodular opacities involving all lobes, but most marked
in the right lower lobe which is new/increased compared to prior and is
concerning for aspiration or broncho pneumonia.

Substantial increase in extent of left lower lobe atelectasis, now mostly
collapsed.

Unchanged large pericardial effusion.

Interstitial septal thickening in the lung apices and bases suggest
interstitial pulmonary edema.

Partly imaged hyperdense lesion along the left kidney.  Dedicated CT or MR may
be helpful to evaluate further although ultraound may prove useful as a
possible way of demonstrating and characterizing a complex cyst, which is more
likely than a solid mass.

EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with infiltrate  // r/o mass

TECHNIQUE:  Non-contrast chest CT was performed acquiring sequential axial
images from the thoracic inlet through the adrenal glands. Thin section axial,
coronal, sagittal and axial MIP's were also obtained.

DOSE:  Total DLP = 454.76mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  ___.

FINDINGS: 

The thyroid gland is unremarkable. Multiple small mediastinal lymph nodes
measuring up to 9 mm in short axis in the right lower paratracheal location
are slightly larger as compared to ___. There are no pathologically
enlarged supraclavicular, mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymph nodes.

Heart size is top-normal with no pericardial effusion. The main pulmonary
artery is normal caliber. The right pulmonary artery is dilated measuring 2.9
cm. The left pulmonary artery is top-normal caliber measuring 2.7 cm.  This is
of uncertain significance. Extensive coronary artery and aortic valvular
calcifications are present. Heavy mitral annular calcifications are also
noted.

Moderate bronchial wall thickening is most extensive in the lower lobes. There
is also circumferential wall thickening diffusely involving the trachea and
mainstem bronchi with presence of a focal calcification in wall a proximal
with main bronchus (4, 104). Secretions layer dependently in the lower trachea
and bilateral main bronchus. Right lower lobe predominant peribronchial
ground-glass opacities are likely infectious or inflammatory in etiology.
Solid pulmonary micronodules measuring no more than 3 mm are stable since ___, and may be presumed benign (4: 35, 37, 46, 95). Upper lobe predominant
centrilobular nodules are again noted. Right middle lobe subpleural
reticulation may be due to a non-characteristic interstitial abnormality.

The lower esophageal wall shows asymmetric nodular wall thickening (4, 186).
Images of the upper abdomen show nonspecific gaseous distension of the stomach
and colon. A small right hepatic lobe cyst is incidentally noted.

Spinal degenerative changes are most extensive in the cervical region. There
are no bone lesions in the thorax worrisome for infection or malignancy. Mild
kyphosis and mild dextroscoliosis of the thoracolumbar spine is unchanged.

IMPRESSION: 

Increased diffuse tracheal and bilateral mainstem bronchial wall thickening
with new focal calcification raises concern for airway involvement by
Wegener's granulomatosis or amyloidosis.

Right lower lobe predominant peribronchial ground-glass opacities may be due
to infection or aspiration.

Upper lobe predominant centrilobular nodules may be due to infection or
respiratory bronchiolitis.

Nodular asymmetric wall thickening of the lower esophagus warrants endoscopic
correlation is advised to exclude a mass.

Extensive coronary artery and aortic valvular calcifications.

communications system for direct communication to the referring provider.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  SECOND OPINION CT TORSO

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with sudden onset dysphagia to solids and
liquids and multifocal numbness. EGD negative for masses and gastritis. ENT
eval negative for upper airway etiology.CT chest with contrast done on ___
at ___ (MRN ___// quality of pulmonary nodule, bilateral hilar
lymphadenopathy, ?distal esophageal thickening

TECHNIQUE:  This is a review of a chest CT that was obtained at an outside
hospital.  CT chest axial images and multiplanar reformats obtained before and
after intravenous contrast administration were submitted for second opinion.

DOSE:  Total DLP: 159 mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS:


NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: The imaged portion of thyroid gland
is mildly heterogeneous with possible hypodensity within the left thyroid
gland.  Scattered supraclavicular lymph nodes are mildly enlarged with a
collection of nodes at the thoracic inlet measuring up to 19 x 16 mm (02:11). 
There is no axillary lymphadenopathy.  Aside from the breast parenchyma, which
is suboptimally evaluated on the current modality, the chest wall is
unremarkable.

UPPER ABDOMEN: The imaged portion of the upper abdomen demonstrate multiple
subcentimeter hypodensities throughout the liver, likely representing cysts or
biliary hamartomas.  Hypodensity that is partially imaged in the midpole of
the right kidney is incompletely evaluated on this exam. There is a small
hiatal hernia.  Residual high-density material within the esophagus is likely
ingested oral contrast.  Otherwise, the imaged upper abdomen is unremarkable.

MEDIASTINUM: There are numerous pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the
mediastinum, measuring up to 10 mm.  The largest lymph nodes are located in
the aortopulmonic window and the subcarinal station (02:17, 18).  Multiple
enlarged lymph nodes are seen adjacent to the esophagus, the largest measuring
up to 10 mm (02:27).

HILA: There is bilateral, symmetric confluent hilar lymphadenopathy.  The
right hilar lymph node measures up to 31 x 21 mm (02:21, 24) and the left
hilar node measures up to 23 x 11 mm (02:18, 20, 24).  The lymphadenopathy
does not narrow the pulmonary vasculature or the bronchi.

HEART and PERICARDIUM: The heart is mildly enlarged in size.  There is no
significant valvular or coronary calcifications.  There is no pericardial
effusion.
PLEURA: There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.  There is mild pleural
thickening in the posteromedial right lower lobe pleura with associated mild
ground-glass opacity (6:170), measuring up to 10 mm.
LUNG:

1. PARENCHYMA:  There is mild biapical scarring.  There are multiple
millimetric subpleural nodules in the left and right upper, right lower lobe
and lingula (6:170, 167, 68, 63, 61, 60, 27, 22), some in peribronchial
distribution.  In the right paraspinal subpleural lesion, there is mildly
spiculated soft tissue thickening adjacent to the pulmonary vessel (6:146),
measuring 15 x 6 mm, which extends downward to the diaphragm.  Inferior to the
right lower lobe prior median soft tissue and ground-glass opacities, there is
a 12 x 8 mm slightly spiculated solid pulmonary nodule (6:189).  The second
largest solid pulmonary nodule measures up to 5 mm in the left upper lobe
(6:61).  The linear opacity in the right middle lobe likely represents
atelectasis (6:170).
2. AIRWAYS:  The airways are patent to the subsegmental levels. There is no
significant peribronchial thickening or irregularity.
3. VESSELS:  Coarse calcification is seen in the descending aorta.  Otherwise,
there is no significant aortic arch calcifications.  There is common origin of
the innominate and left common carotid artery.  The main and right pulmonary
arteries are normal in caliber.  The ascending and descending aorta are not
aneurysmal.
CHEST CAGE: There are no worrisome osseous lesions for infection or
malignancy.

IMPRESSION:


-Confluent mediastinal and symmetric bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with
peribronchial nodules, suspicious for sarcoidosis.
-Slightly spiculated solid pulmonary nodule in the right lower lobe measuring
up to 12 mm.  This is likely be part of the spectrum of sarcoidosis and less
likely lymphoma or small cell lung cancer.  However, transbronchial biopsy and
tissue diagnosis would be helpful for definitive clinical management.
-Multiple enlarged lymph nodes adjacent to the esophagus, which is mildly
enlarged.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Slightly spiculated solid pulmonary nodule in the right
lower lobe measuring up to 12 mm. This is likely be part of the spectrum of
sarcoidosis and less likely lymphoma or small cell lung cancer. However,
transbronchial biopsy and tissue diagnosis would be helpful for definitive
clinical management.

NOTIFICATION:   The findings were discussed with ___. by
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 10:18 am, 10 minutes after
discovery of the findings.

administration of intravenous contrast.  Axial coronal and sagittal
reconstructions were acquired.  Maximum intensity projections were also
acquired

mGy-cm.

THORACIC INLET: The thyroid is unremarkable.  There are no enlarged
supraclavicular lymph nodes

BREAST AND AXILLA  : There are no enlarged axillary lymph nodes

MEDIASTINUM: There are multiple small mediastinal lymph nodes.  A right
paratracheal node measures 13 mm.  The subcarinal node measures 9 mm.  There
are small right pericardial lymph nodes.  The largest measuring 6 mm.  A small
left pericardial node measures 3 mm.  The heart, aorta and pulmonary artery
normal in caliber.  There is a small hiatus hernia
.  There is no pericardial effusion
PLEURA: There are small bilateral effusions the left is trace volume the right
is partially loculated and chronic.

LUNG: Consolidative opacity in the right lower lobe most likely represents
round atelectasis.  Bands of atelectasis are seen in the right upper lobe. 
There is a calcified granuloma in the right upper lobe.  The interstitium is
prominent.  There is a 2 mm calcified granuloma in the right upper lobe (2,
16).  There is a 2 mm non calcified nodule in the left upper lobe (2, 29).  Is

limits evaluation of the liver.

radiology_facts.report_text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with acute on chronic anemia after car accident
req 3+ blood transfusions.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT axial images were acquired through the chestwithout the
administration of intravenous contrast.Coronal, sagittal, and MIP reformations
were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Total DLP (Body) = 1,768 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS W/O CONTRAST)

COMPARISON:  CT chest from ___

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE, CHEST WALL: The thyroid gland is unremarkable. 
There are no pathologically enlarged supraclavicular or axillary lymph nodes.

UPPER ABDOMEN: Please see the separate report for the same day CT abdomen and
pelvis for subdiaphragmatic findings.

MEDIASTINUM: There are numerous mediastinal lymph nodes which have increased
in size compared to prior including a 1.1 cm upper right paratracheal lymph
node and a 1.3 cm lower right paratracheal lymph node which were previously
subcentimeter, and a prevascular lymph node which measures 9 mm, previously 5
mm.

HILA: Calcified left hilar lymph nodes are unchanged compared to prior.  There
is no hilar adenopathy.

HEART and PERICARDIUM: Heart size is normal.  No pericardial effusion. 
Minimal coronary artery calcifications are noted.

PLEURA: Small pleural effusions again noted bilaterally, with partial
loculation on the right again noted.  No pneumothorax.

LUNG:

1. PARENCHYMA:  There is no pulmonary mass.  Focal ground-glass opacification
in the right middle lobe likely represents the sequelae of aspiration
(302:125).  There is increased right basilar atelectasis.  Areas of rounded
atelectasis in the right lower lung are similar to prior.  A 2 mm ground-glass
nodule left upper lobe is new, likely inflammatory (302:90).  Two solid 2 mm
pulmonary nodules in the left upper lobe are unchanged (302:101).  Scattered
calcified granulomas are unchanged (302: 41, 66, 117, 163, 182).
2. AIRWAYS:  The central airways are patent.  There is mild bronchial wall
thickening.
3. VESSELS:  The thoracic aorta and main pulmonary artery are of normal
caliber.

CHEST CAGE: There are acute nondisplaced fractures of the right first anterior
rib and the anterolateral right fourth, fifth, and seventh ribs.  There are
mildly displaced fractures of the right anterolateral second and third ribs
and acute fractures through the second and third costochondral junctions.

IMPRESSION:


1. Multiple acute right-sided anterior rib fractures spanning the first
through fifth and seventh ribs, detailed above.
2. Bilateral small pleural effusions, with partially loculated right pleural
effusion with subjacent areas of rounded atelectasis and scarring.
3. Ground-glass opacification in the right middle lobe and a small
ground-glass nodule in the left upper lobe, likely the sequelae of
inflammation/aspiration.
4. Mediastinal nodal prominence, likely reactive.

radiology_facts.report_text:
COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY OF THE THORAX

INDICATION:  Lung opacity on chest x-ray, status post thoracocentesis,
exudative pleural effusion.  Evaluation of left lung opacity.

COMPARISON:  No comparison available at the time of dictation.

TECHNIQUE:  Volumetric CT acquisitions over the entire thorax in inspiration,
with administration of intravenous contrast material, multiplanar
reconstructions.

FINDINGS:  No incidental thyroid findings.  No supraclavicular,
infraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy.  The lymph nodes seen in these
regions have normal size.  No enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes.  No enlarged
lymph nodes along the large mediastinal vessels.

Minimal aortic wall calcifications, minimal coronary calcifications. 
Otherwise, the coronary and mediastinal vessels are unremarkable.

Normal size of the heart.

Moderate perihepatic ascites.  Multiple hypodense rounded hepatic lesions. 
Mild splenomegaly.  Mild hepatomegaly.  Numerous borderline lymph nodes around
the celiac trunk.

No evidence of osteolytic lesions.

Moderate left-sided pleural effusion.  No right-sided pleural effusion.

The assessment of the lung parenchyma is limited by severe motion artifacts
caused by respiration.  In the left lung, both in the upper and lower lobe, a
diffuse and predominantly alveolar peribronchial pattern of opacity is seen. 
The patterns display multiple air bronchograms and show a coexisting mild
thickening of the interstitial components.  Parts of the opacities have a
crazy paving appearance.

No major atelectatic components are seen in the left lung.  In the right lung,
there is an area of mild paraseptal emphysema (4, 112).  Paraseptal emphysema
is also present around the right hilus (4, 120).  Non-characteristic anterior
subpleural 5-mm ground-glass opacity in the right upper lobe (4, 63).  Minimal
apical centrilobular emphysema.

Otherwise, no abnormalities are noted in the lung parenchyma on the right.

IMPRESSION:  Extensive left-sided partly alveolar partly interstitial opacity,
diffusely distributed virtually to all parts of the left lung.  Accompanying
pleural effusion of moderate extent with the intrafissural component.  The
distribution and presence of a pleural effusion is suggestive of an infectious
disease.  The suspicion should be further explored using bronchoalveolar
lavage.  There is no evidence of a circumscribed mass or nodule.

Subtle emphysematous changes in the right lung, no other changes on the right.

No hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

Mild perihepatic ascites, hepatosplenomegaly.  Borderline sized lymph nodes
around the celiac trunk.
